source: CIS AWS DATABASE SERVICES BENCHMARK V1
title: Ensure to Implement Access Control and Authentication

input_row.source: CIS AWS DATABASE SERVICES BENCHMARK V1
input_row.title: Ensure to Implement Access Control and Authentication
input_row.description: Users should select whether they like to enable authentication. If they want to authenticate a password would be required, which would only allow the authorized person to access the database. Defining access control allows specific workers in a business access to the database.
input_row.audit: 1. Sign into the AWS Management Console • Sign into the AWS Management Console at https://console.aws.amazon.com/ with your AWS account credentials. 2. Open the Amazon RDS Console • Navigate to the service using the Find Services search bar or by directly accessing the console at https://console.aws.amazon.com/rds/. 3. Select the RDS Instance • Choose the Amazon RDS instance you want to implement access control and authentication. • Click on the instance name to access its details page. • In the instance details page, navigate to the Configuration or Connectivity & Security section. 4. Enable IAM Database Authentication • Under the Connectivity or Connectivity & Security section. • Click the Modify or Edit option to enable IAM Database Authentication. • Select the option to enable IAM Database Authentication. • Click Continue or Save to apply the changes. 5. Create and Configure IAM Database Users • Click Users in the left-side menu in the Amazon RDS console. • Click Create database user to create a new IAM database user.  • Provide a username and select the IAM role or IAM user that will be associated with the database user. • Configure the authentication type, either Password-based or IAM authentication. • Set the desired password or leave it blank for IAM authentication. • Configure the database user's privileges and permissions based on your application's requirements. • Click Create to create the IAM database user. 6. Configure Database User Privileges • Click Users in the left-side menu in the Amazon RDS console. • Select the database user you created in the previous step. • Click on Modify to modify the user's settings and permissions. • Configure the user's access privileges, including database access, object permissions, and privileges. • Click Save or Apply Changes to update the user's privileges. 7. Test Access and Authentication • Test the access and authentication by connecting to the RDS instance using the IAM database user's credentials or IAM role. • Verify that the authentication and access control mechanisms are functioning correctly. 8. Monitor and Manage IAM Database Users • Regularly monitor and review the IAM database users and their access privileges. • Adjust user privileges as needed based on changes in your application requirements. • Remove or disable database users when they are no longer needed.

step1_initial_assessment:
**AUDIT APPROACH:** Manual

**APPROACH DECISION:**
- This control requires the auditor to validate not only whether access control (authentication) is enabled on the RDS instance, but also how users are created/assigned, privileges set, and ensuring only authorized identities can connect.
- The "audit" steps in the benchmark are entirely console-based, describing reviewing enabled authentication methods, configuring IAM authentication, user privilege management, and actual login testing—all of which require human interpretation and in some cases, cross-verification against business requirements.
- While some technical aspects (like verifying that IAM authentication is enabled) can be checked via AWS APIs (boto3: describe_db_instances), much of this control refers to reviewing user privileges and connectivity methods, as well as confirming that authentication is enforced for all access—areas not fully covered by AWS APIs (e.g., user-level privilege assignments inside the database engine, testing connectivity, and ensuring strong authentication practices).
- Creation, management, and privilege assignment of database users (besides IAM authentication) happen at the database engine level and are not typically exposed by AWS APIs; confirming appropriate access/privilege configuration requires DB admin-level access or application review.
- Therefore, the nature of this control is broader than a simple API-checkable property and requires manual validation.

---

**MANUAL STEPS:**

1. **Review RDS Instance Authentication Method:**
   - In the AWS Console, go to the RDS service and select the target database instance.
   - Under the "Configuration" or "Connectivity & Security" tab, check if "IAM Database Authentication" is enabled.
   - Confirm if this setting aligns with your organization's requirements (preferably enabled for enhanced security).

2. **Review Database Users and Authentication Type:**
   - In the AWS Console (for RDS/console-supported DB engines only), navigate to the "Users" section within the database instance.
   - Review each database user's authentication type (password-based, IAM authentication).
   - Confirm that only authorized users are present and all users utilize strong authentication mechanisms.

3. **Verify User Privileges:**
   - For each user, review and document the assigned privileges (database access rights, object permissions).
   - Confirm that privileges follow the principle of least privilege (users only have access necessary for their job function).

4. **Test Authentication Mechanism:**
   - Attempt to connect to the RDS instance using the credentials or IAM role for each user type.
   - Confirm that unauthorized access is denied, and only properly authenticated users can connect.
   - Validate that authentication and access controls work as intended.

5. **Monitor and Review Regularly:**
   - Review the user list and privileges periodically.
   - Adjust or remove user access as needed according to organizational changes and security policies.

6. **Document Findings:**
   - Record results of each step above for audit evidence, including screenshots or exported lists of users/privileges if possible.

**SOURCE:** CIS AWS DATABASE SERVICES BENCHMARK V1
